walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Ark Nova set up. First, unfold the main board and place it in the center of the table. This large board has several tracks and areas on it. You will see the Appeal track running along the top and left side, the Conservation track running along the bottom and right side, and the Break track in the corner. There is also the Association board section and the card display area. Make sure everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Card Display
walkthrough.text: Now let's prepare the card display. Shuffle all 212 zoo cards into one face-down deck and place it on the designated spot on the main board. Then deal six cards face up into the numbered display slots, from position one through position six. These cards will be available for players to pick up during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Association Board
walkthrough.text: Find the Association board and place it next to the main board. This is where players will send workers to partner zoos, universities, and conservation projects. Place the available conservation project cards face up in their designated slots. The Association board is one of the most important areas in the game, so make sure it is visible to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Tokens and Money
walkthrough.text: Let's organize the supply. Sort all the money tokens by denomination and place them in a shared supply. Set out the X-tokens in their own pile nearby. Also sort out the various bonus tokens, partner zoo tiles, and university tiles. Keep everything accessible in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Enclosure Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find all the standard enclosure tiles and sort them by size. You will see sizes one through five, each showing different shapes. Also locate the special enclosure tiles: the petting zoo and the reptile house. Place all enclosure tiles in organized piles near the main board where everyone can see the available options.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Final Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the final scoring cards and deal two to each player. Each player looks at their two cards and keeps one, discarding the other face down. These cards give bonus points at the end of the game based on specific conditions. Keep your chosen card secret from other players.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Zoo Maps
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one zoo map board. These double-sided boards show your personal zoo layout with a grid of spaces where you will build enclosures. For your first game, everyone should use the same recommended side, which is labeled with a number one. Place your zoo map in front of you with plenty of room on all sides.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Enclosures
walkthrough.text: Each zoo map comes with a few starting enclosures already printed on the board. These are the spaces you can place animals in right away without needing to build first. Notice how each enclosure has a size number. You will need to match animal sizes to enclosure sizes throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Action Cards
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of Ark Nova's action system. Each player takes five action cards: Cards, Build, Animals, Association, and Sponsors. Arrange them in a row below your zoo map in slots numbered one through five. Each card starts on its side one, which is the basic version. The slot number determines the action's strength when you play it.

walkthrough.title: Place Player Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes two markers in their chosen color. Place one marker on the starting position of the Appeal track and the other on the starting position of the Conservation track on the main board. Also place your break marker on the break track. These markers will move throughout the game as you gain appeal and contribute to conservation.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player receives 25 money from the supply. Also, each player takes one X-token. Finally, deal eight zoo cards from the deck to each player. Players look at their cards, keep a hand of cards, and place the rest on the bottom of the deck. The exact number you keep depends on player count.

walkthrough.title: Place Association Workers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their association worker meeples. You start with a set number of workers that you can send out to partner zoos, universities, and conservation projects throughout the game. Place them near your zoo map where you can easily reach them.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player randomly. In Ark Nova, there is no strict turn order after the first player is determined. Players simply take turns clockwise. The starting player gets a slight disadvantage in starting money to balance going first, so check the rulebook for the exact starting money adjustment per seat position.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. You should have the main board with its tracks and card display in the center, the Association board nearby, and each player should have their zoo map, five action cards in slots one through five, starting money, an X-token, and their hand of zoo cards. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: In Ark Nova, you are building a modern zoo that balances two goals: making your zoo attractive to visitors and contributing to wildlife conservation. On your turn, you play one of your five action cards. The game has no fixed rounds. Instead, players keep taking turns until the Appeal marker and Conservation marker cross paths on the intertwined scoring track, which triggers the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: The Action Card System
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most unique part of Ark Nova. You have five action cards arranged in slots one through five. The slot number is the action's strength. When you play an action card, you use it at its slot's strength, then move it down to slot one. All cards that were below it shift up by one slot, becoming stronger. This means the longer you wait to use an action, the more powerful it becomes.

walkthrough.title: X-Tokens Boost Strength
walkthrough.text: X-tokens are wild tokens that can do three things. First, you can spend one to boost any action's strength by one. Second, you can use one as a substitute for two money when paying costs. Third, some card abilities specifically require X-tokens. You start with one X-token and can earn more throughout the game. They are very flexible and valuable, so spend them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Action: Cards
walkthrough.text: The strength determines how many cards you can take. You may draw from the face-up display or the top of the deck in any combination. When you take a card from the display, slide remaining cards toward position six and refill from the deck. There is a hand limit, so you may need to discard down after drawing.

walkthrough.title: Snapping Cards
walkthrough.text: Here is a clever trick. Whenever a card slides off position six of the display because new cards push it out, any player can snap that card by paying one money. This happens immediately, even on another player's turn. Snapping is a great way to grab cards cheaply, but you have to pay attention to the display at all times.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build
walkthrough.text: The strength determines the maximum size of enclosure you can build. You pay money equal to the enclosure's cost, pick a tile of the appropriate size, and place it on your zoo map. Enclosures must fit on the grid and cannot overlap existing enclosures or other structures. Special enclosures like the petting zoo and reptile house have their own rules.

walkthrough.title: Zoo Map Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Your zoo map has bonus icons printed on certain spaces. When you cover a bonus icon with an enclosure tile, you immediately gain that bonus. Bonuses can include money, reputation, X-tokens, or other rewards. Planning your enclosure placement to hit valuable bonuses is a key part of the strategy.

walkthrough.title: Action: Animals
walkthrough.text: The strength determines the maximum size of animal you can play. You must pay the animal's money cost, and the animal's size must match an available empty enclosure. When you play an animal, you gain appeal equal to the animal's appeal value and move your Appeal marker up the track.

walkthrough.title: Animal Requirements
walkthrough.text: Many animal cards have additional requirements beyond just size and cost. Some require specific enclosure types like a reptile house or petting zoo. Some require you to have a partner zoo from the animal's continent. Others need a certain reputation level. Always check the full requirements on the card before planning to play an animal.

walkthrough.title: Icon Synergies
walkthrough.text: Animals have icons showing their continent and animal type. The five continents are Africa, Europe, Asia, the Americas, and Australia. Animal types include primates, reptiles, birds, predators, and herbivores among others. Many cards and bonuses reward you for collecting sets of matching icons. Building around a continent or animal type theme can trigger powerful combo bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Action: Association
walkthrough.text: The strength determines how many tasks you can perform. Tasks include partnering with a zoo on a specific continent, sending a worker to a university for ongoing bonuses, or contributing to a conservation project. Conservation projects are especially important because they advance your Conservation marker, and conservation is half of your final score.

walkthrough.title: Conservation Projects
walkthrough.text: These are cards on the Association board that require you to meet certain conditions, like having animals from a specific continent or of a specific type. When you contribute to a project, you advance your Conservation marker and often gain bonus rewards. The Conservation track is just as important as the Appeal track because your final score depends on both.

walkthrough.title: Action: Sponsors
walkthrough.text: The strength determines the maximum cost level of sponsor you can play. Sponsors provide ongoing effects, immediate bonuses, or end-game scoring. Some sponsors give you income during breaks, others reduce costs, and some grant powerful one-time abilities. Sponsors do not require enclosures and are played directly to your area.

walkthrough.title: The Reputation Track
walkthrough.text: This measures your zoo's prestige. You gain reputation from various actions and card effects. When you reach certain thresholds on the reputation track, you unlock the ability to upgrade one of your five action cards from side one to side two. Upgraded action cards are significantly more powerful, so pushing your reputation to reach these thresholds is a major priority.

walkthrough.title: Upgrading Action Cards
walkthrough.text: When you reach a reputation threshold, you immediately flip one action card from side one to side two. The upgraded version gives you much stronger effects at every strength level. For example, the upgraded Animals card might let you play larger animals or gain extra appeal. Choosing which card to upgrade first is a critical strategic decision that shapes the rest of your game.

walkthrough.title: The Break System
walkthrough.text: Ark Nova does not have rounds, but it does have breaks. The break token advances whenever certain events happen, such as the card display refilling or specific card effects triggering. When the break token reaches the end of its track, a break occurs. During a break, all players receive income based on their appeal level and must pay upkeep costs. Then the break token resets.

walkthrough.title: Income and Upkeep
walkthrough.text: During a break, you receive income based on your position on the Appeal track. Higher appeal means more money coming in. You also pay upkeep for your animals and any ongoing costs. The difference between income and upkeep is your net gain. Early in the game your income is low, but as you add animals and gain appeal, your income grows substantially.

walkthrough.title: Releasing Animals
walkthrough.text: One powerful option is releasing animals back into the wild. When you release an animal from your zoo, you lose the appeal it provided but gain a significant boost on the Conservation track. This represents your zoo's contribution to species preservation. Releasing is a strategic trade-off between appeal and conservation progress.

walkthrough.title: The Appeal and Conservation Tracks
walkthrough.text: These two tracks run in opposite directions around the main board and intertwine at a crossing point. Your Appeal marker moves up as you play animals and sponsors. Your Conservation marker moves up as you complete conservation projects and release animals. The game ends immediately when any player's Appeal and Conservation markers meet or cross on the intertwined track.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: When a player's markers cross, the current round does not finish. The game ends immediately. All other players get to count where their markers are. This means you need to keep an eye on all players' positions because the game can end suddenly when someone pushes their markers together.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Your final score is calculated by taking your Appeal position and subtracting your Conservation position. Wait, that sounds backwards, so let's be precise. The tracks are numbered so that where your markers cross gives you a positive score. The further past the crossing point your markers have gone, the higher your score. Then add any bonuses from your final scoring card. The highest score wins.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreakers
walkthrough.text: If two or more players have the same final score, the tiebreaker is the player with the most money remaining. If there is still a tie, the player with the most cards left in hand wins. These tiebreakers rarely come into play, but keep some money in reserve just in case it is close.

Final score = position where markers cross + final scoring card bonuses

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your Zoo
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with two players. Player one will be building an Africa-themed zoo, and Player two will focus on a reptile-heavy strategy. Follow along with the moves and we will point out common mistakes along the way.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Your Action Slots
walkthrough.text: Remember, the slot number is the action's strength. Right now each card is at a random starting position. The key insight is that after you use a card, it drops to slot one and everything else shifts up. So if you use the card in slot five, you get a powerful action, but that card resets to the weakest position.

walkthrough.title: Player One: First Action - Cards
walkthrough.text: Player one, let's start by drawing cards. Your Cards action is sitting in slot three, so you can draw three cards. Look at the six face-up cards in the display and pick ones that match your Africa theme. Let's grab two African animals and one sponsor card that rewards continent bonuses. Slide your Cards action down to slot one and shift the other cards up.

walkthrough.title: Why Drawing First Is Smart
walkthrough.text: Drawing cards early gives you options. You need animals in hand before you can play them, and you want to see what is available before committing to a building plan. Many new players rush to build enclosures before they even know what animals they want to house. Having cards in hand helps you plan your next several turns.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: First Action - Build
walkthrough.text: Player two, let's build an enclosure. Your Build action is in slot two, so you can build up to a size two enclosure. Pay the money cost and place a size two enclosure tile on your zoo map. Try to cover a bonus icon if possible. Now slide Build down to slot one and shift the others up.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Building Without a Plan
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about Player two's first potential mistake. They built a size two enclosure, which is fine, but they did not check whether they actually have a size two animal in hand to fill it. If you build an enclosure and have no matching animal, that enclosure sits empty, wasting money and a turn. The enclosure earns you nothing until an animal is inside it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Match Enclosures to Animals
walkthrough.text: Before you build, check your hand. Do you have an animal that fits the enclosure size you are about to construct? Building a size four enclosure when your biggest animal is a size two means you spent a lot of money on empty space. Always build with a specific animal in mind, not just because you have the money to do it.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Play an Animal
walkthrough.text: Player one, your Animals action has shifted up to slot four. That is strong enough to play a size four animal, but let's start small. Play a size two African animal into one of your starting enclosures. Pay its money cost and move your Appeal marker up by the animal's appeal value. Nice work. You are on the board with your first exhibit.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Draw Cards
walkthrough.text: Your Cards action has shifted up since you used Build last turn. Draw cards from the display, looking specifically for reptiles or sponsor cards that synergize with reptile houses. Now you have options for your next few turns.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Association Action
walkthrough.text: Send a worker to partner with an African zoo. This gives you a partner zoo tile for Africa, which will help you meet requirements on your African animal cards. Many African animals require an Africa partner zoo before you can play them. Getting this early opens up your options.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Conservation
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and look at both players' Conservation markers. They are both still sitting at zero. This is a classic beginner trap. New players focus entirely on building enclosures and playing animals because those feel productive, but they forget that conservation is literally half of your final score. If your Appeal marker races ahead but your Conservation marker barely moves, you will lose.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Conservation Is Half Your Score
walkthrough.text: The game ends when your Appeal and Conservation markers cross. If you push appeal to 80 but conservation is stuck at five, your markers will never cross, and other players will end the game while you are still struggling. You need to advance both tracks. Start thinking about conservation projects early, not just in the last few turns when it is too late to catch up.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Play an Animal
walkthrough.text: Pay the money cost and gain appeal. Your zoo is starting to take shape. Notice how your reptile has icons for both its continent and animal type. Keep an eye on these icons because they will matter for conservation projects and sponsor bonuses later.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Sponsors Action
walkthrough.text: Player one, let's play a sponsor card. Your Sponsors action is in a decent slot. Play the sponsor card that rewards you for having multiple animals from the same continent. Since you are building an Africa theme, this sponsor will get more valuable as you add more African animals. Sponsors do not need enclosures, so they are efficient plays.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Always Playing at Low Strength
walkthrough.text: Let's examine a subtle mistake that new players make constantly. Player two has been using whichever action card happens to be in their highest slot, regardless of whether that action is useful right now. They played Build at strength two, then Cards at strength three. But they never let any action climb to slot four or five before using it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Cycle Cards for Power
walkthrough.text: The action card system rewards patience. If you always grab whatever is in slot four or five, you end up taking mediocre actions at high strength. Instead, deliberately use your less important actions at low strength to let your key actions climb higher. Need a big Animals play? Use Cards and Build at low strength for a couple turns to push Animals up to slot five. Cycling is the secret to powerful turns.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Association for Conservation
walkthrough.text: Use your Association action to contribute to a conservation project. You need animals of a specific type or from a specific continent. Check which projects you qualify for based on the animals you have played so far. Complete one and watch your Conservation marker jump forward.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Building Strategically
walkthrough.text: Player one, you need a bigger enclosure for your next African animal. Your Build action has been climbing while you used other actions. Now it is in slot four, so you can build up to a size four enclosure. Place it on your zoo map, covering a bonus icon for extra reputation. That reputation gets you closer to upgrading an action card.

walkthrough.title: The Upgrade Moment
walkthrough.text: Player one just hit a reputation threshold. This means you can flip one action card from side one to side two. Let's upgrade the Animals card, since your strategy revolves around playing African animals. The upgraded Animals card gives you extra appeal or lets you play larger animals more efficiently. This is a game-changing moment that accelerates your engine.

walkthrough.title: Break Time
walkthrough.text: The break token has reached the end of its track, triggering a break. Both players receive income based on their Appeal track position. Player one, with more animals and higher appeal, receives more money. Both players pay upkeep costs. After income and upkeep, the break token resets and play continues normally.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check-In
walkthrough.text: Let's assess both players. Player one has three animals, a sponsor, a partner zoo, and has upgraded one action card. Player two has two animals and one conservation project completed. Both players should now be thinking about how to push their Conservation markers forward while continuing to build appeal. The race to cross the tracks is on.

walkthrough.title: Putting It All Together
walkthrough.text: From here, the game follows the same patterns. Draw cards to find animals and sponsors that match your strategy. Build enclosures that fit the animals you want to play. Play animals for appeal and icon synergies. Use the Association action for partner zoos and conservation projects. Play sponsors for ongoing bonuses. Keep pushing both Appeal and Conservation markers toward the crossing point.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all five actions in play, learned how the action card cycling system works, and spotted three common mistakes. Remember to build enclosures you can actually fill, do not ignore conservation until it is too late, and cycle your action cards deliberately to get powerful turns when you need them.

walkthrough.title: Before You Open Your Zoo
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to action cards, icons, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Match enclosures to animals, prioritize conservation, cycle cards deliberately

summary.title: Before You Open Your Zoo
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick guide to actions, icons, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Ark Nova players make. First, building enclosures you cannot fill wastes money and board space. Second, ignoring the Conservation track until the last few turns leaves you unable to end the game. Third, always playing actions at low strength instead of cycling cards to push important actions higher. Fourth, spending all your money early and having nothing left when a perfect animal shows up. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Action Card Cycling
walkthrough.text: The action card system is what makes Ark Nova unique, and mastering it separates beginners from experienced players. The key principle is deliberate cycling. Do not just grab whatever action happens to be in your highest slot. Instead, plan two or three turns ahead. If you know you want a big Animals play next turn, use your less critical actions at low strength this turn to push Animals up to slot four or five. Think of your action card row as a conveyor belt that you control.

walkthrough.title: Balance Appeal and Conservation
walkthrough.text: Your final score depends on both the Appeal and Conservation tracks. New players tend to focus heavily on appeal because playing animals feels satisfying and productive. But conservation is equally important. A good rule of thumb is to complete your first conservation project by mid-game and have at least two or three completed by the end. Start partnering with zoos and universities early so you have the prerequisites when good conservation projects appear.

walkthrough.title: Money Management
walkthrough.text: Money is tight in Ark Nova, especially in the early game before your income ramps up. Resist the urge to spend everything on a big animal or a large enclosure in the first few turns. Keep a reserve of at least five to eight money so you can snap a great card from the display or respond to unexpected opportunities. As your appeal grows, your break income increases, but early game austerity pays off.

walkthrough.title: Build a Theme
walkthrough.text: Ark Nova rewards specialization more than generalization. Pick a continent or animal type early and build around it. Africa-themed zoos, reptile-heavy collections, or primate-focused strategies all create powerful synergies with sponsors, conservation projects, and partner zoos. A zoo with five African animals and an Africa partner zoo generates far more value than five random animals from five different continents.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Timing
walkthrough.text: Reaching reputation thresholds to upgrade action cards is a major inflection point. The upgraded side two cards are dramatically stronger than side one. Prioritize gaining reputation early so you can upgrade at least one card by mid-game. The best first upgrade depends on your strategy, but the Animals and Association cards are common choices. An upgraded Animals card lets you play bigger animals more efficiently, while an upgraded Association card gives you more conservation power per action.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Card Display
walkthrough.text: The six-card display is the lifeblood of Ark Nova. Get in the habit of scanning it every turn, even when it is not your turn. Watch for animals that fit your theme, sponsors that synergize with your strategy, and conservation projects that match your animals. Pay special attention to position six because cards sliding off can be snapped for just one money. Some of the best deals in the game come from well-timed snaps.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Awareness
walkthrough.text: The game ends suddenly when any player's Appeal and Conservation markers cross. This means you need to watch all players' positions, not just your own. If an opponent is close to crossing, you might need to rush your own scoring rather than setting up one more perfect turn. Conversely, if you are close to crossing, look for the fastest way to push your markers together, even if it means taking a slightly suboptimal action.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't build enclosures you can't fill — wasted money/space
Don't ignore Conservation track — it's half your final score
Don't always play actions at low strength — cycle cards deliberately
Don't spend all money early — keep 5-8 in reserve for opportunities

High appeal + low conservation = markers never cross

walkthrough.title: The Continent Engine
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest strategies in Ark Nova is building around a single continent. Pick Africa, Asia, or another continent early and go deep. Partner with that continent's zoo as your first Association action. Then collect animals exclusively from that continent. This approach unlocks continent-specific conservation projects, activates sponsor bonuses that scale with your continent count, and makes your card draws more efficient because you know exactly what you are looking for.

walkthrough.title: Conservation Rush
walkthrough.text: The conservation rush strategy prioritizes the Conservation track aggressively. Instead of building a big appealing zoo first, focus on getting partner zoos, universities, and completing conservation projects early. Release animals when it advances your Conservation marker significantly. This approach can catch opponents off guard because you can end the game quickly while they are still building their engine. The key risk is low income from low appeal, so you need to be efficient with money.

walkthrough.title: Sponsor Synergy Chains
walkthrough.text: Sponsors are often undervalued by new players, but a well-timed sponsor chain can be devastating. Look for sponsors that trigger off actions you are already taking. A sponsor that gives you one money every time you play an animal effectively discounts all future animals. Stack two or three sponsors that reward the same behavior, and suddenly your routine turns generate massive bonus value. The Sponsors action itself is cheap and does not require enclosures, making it very action-efficient.

walkthrough.title: X-Token Economy
walkthrough.text: X-tokens are the most flexible resource in Ark Nova, and advanced players accumulate and spend them deliberately. Use X-tokens to boost critical actions from slot four to an effective strength of five. Save one X-token for a crucial moment rather than spending it as two money early. Some of the most powerful turns in the game come from boosting an already-high action by one to reach a threshold you otherwise could not hit.

walkthrough.title: Map Optimization
walkthrough.text: Your zoo map is a spatial puzzle with hidden depth. Advanced players plan their entire enclosure layout before placing the first tile. Study the bonus icons on your map and plan a path that covers the most valuable ones. Leave space for large enclosures near high-value bonuses. Use the petting zoo and reptile house strategically because their unique shapes can fill awkward gaps. A well-planned map generates three to five bonus rewards over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Priority Order
walkthrough.text: Choosing which action card to upgrade first is one of the most impactful decisions in the game. For an animal-heavy strategy, upgrade Animals first to play bigger creatures and gain extra appeal. For a conservation rush, upgrade Association to get more tasks per action. For a card-draw engine, upgrade Cards to see more of the deck. The general rule is to upgrade the action you use most frequently first, because the upgraded version compounds across every future use.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Game End
walkthrough.text: Advanced players do not just play their own game. They watch all opponents' track positions and control when the game ends. If you are ahead, push to end the game before opponents can catch up. If you are behind, stall by not advancing your own tracks too quickly and focus on building a big scoring turn. The ability to control game pace by managing your own Appeal and Conservation advancement is what separates competitive Ark Nova players from casual ones.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Display
walkthrough.text: The card display is shared information that tells a story. Watch what cards your opponents take and snap. If someone is collecting European animals, the European conservation projects become more valuable to them and potentially contested. If a powerful sponsor sits untaken in position five, it will slide toward position six soon, creating a cheap snap opportunity. Advanced players read the display like a poker table, inferring opponents' strategies and timing their own draws to maximize value while denying key cards to others.

summary.title: The Continent Engine
bullets: Go deep on 1 continent: partner zoo → animals → conservation projects
Continent focus unlocks sponsor bonuses that scale with count
Focused card draws are more efficient — know what you're looking for
Africa and Asia have the most card support

summary.title: Conservation Rush
bullets: Prioritize Conservation track aggressively over Appeal
Partner zoos + universities + conservation projects early
Release animals for Conservation boosts when efficient
Can end game quickly while opponents are still building

summary.title: Sponsor Synergy Chains

Sponsors action is cheap, no enclosure needed = action-efficient
Often undervalued by new players

entries.definition: One of the five action cards. Lets you draw zoo cards from the display or deck. Strength determines how many cards you draw. On side II, you may also gain additional benefits like snapping cards for free or looking at more options.

One of the five action cards. Lets you build an enclosure on your zoo map. Strength determines the maximum enclosure size you can build. On side II, you gain discounts or can build special enclosures more efficiently.

One of the five action cards. Lets you play an animal card from your hand into an empty enclosure. Strength determines the maximum animal size you can play. On side II, you may gain extra appeal or play animals at reduced cost.

One of the five action cards. Lets you send workers to partner zoos, universities, or conservation projects. Strength determines how many tasks you can perform. On side II, you gain more tasks or more powerful effects per task.

One of the five action cards. Lets you play a sponsor card from your hand. Strength determines the maximum level of sponsor you can play. On side II, you may gain bonus effects when playing sponsors or play higher-level sponsors.
The starting side of each action card, showing basic abilities. All action cards begin on side I and can be upgraded to side II when you reach reputation thresholds.

The upgraded side of each action card, showing significantly more powerful abilities at every strength level. Unlocked by reaching reputation thresholds. Choosing which card to upgrade first is a critical strategic decision.
The power level of an action, determined by the slot number (1-5) where the action card sits. Higher slot = stronger action. Can be boosted by +1 with an X-token.

A zoo card representing an animal species. Shows the animal's size, cost, appeal value, continent icon, animal type icon, and any special requirements or abilities. Played into enclosures on your zoo map.

A zoo card representing a corporate or institutional partner. Provides ongoing effects, immediate bonuses, or end-game scoring. Played directly to your area without needing an enclosure. Strength of the Sponsors action limits which sponsors you can play.

A card on the Association board representing a wildlife conservation initiative. Requires meeting specific conditions (animal types, continents, etc.) to contribute. Completing a project advances the Conservation track and often grants bonus rewards.
A secret card kept from setup that awards bonus points at game end for meeting a specific condition. Each player keeps one of two dealt cards. Revealed during final scoring.

The primary currency in Ark Nova. Used to build enclosures, play animals, play sponsors, and snap cards. Earned through break income (based on Appeal), card effects, and map bonuses. Always public information.

A versatile wild token. Can boost action strength by +1, substitute for 2 money, or fulfill specific card requirements. Start with one; earn more through map bonuses and card effects. Very flexible and valuable.

A track measuring your zoo's prestige. Gained from various actions and card effects. Reaching thresholds unlocks action card upgrades (side I to side II). Pushing reputation early for upgrades is a key strategy.

Meeples sent to the Association board to perform tasks. Used to partner with zoos, attend universities, and contribute to conservation projects. Limited supply — managing where and when to send workers is strategically important.

A tile gained by sending a worker to a continent's zoo on the Association board. Having a partner zoo from a specific continent is often a prerequisite for playing animals from that continent. Also helps qualify for conservation projects.
A space on the Association board where workers can be sent. Universities provide ongoing bonuses such as reputation, money, or other benefits. Each university slot can only be used by one player.

A goal on the Association board requiring specific conditions (continent icons, animal types, etc.). Contributing advances the Conservation track. Multiple players can contribute to the same project. Projects are the primary way to advance conservation.

An Association board action where you release an animal from your zoo back into the wild. You lose the animal's appeal but gain a significant Conservation track boost. Represents your zoo's contribution to species preservation in the wild.
Enclosure tiles sized 1 through 5 that you build on your zoo map. Each enclosure holds one animal whose size matches the enclosure size. Enclosures cost money to build and cannot overlap on the grid.

A special enclosure type with a unique shape. Can hold specific small animals that require a petting zoo. Has its own building rules and placement restrictions. Counts as a special enclosure, not a standard one.

A special enclosure type that can hold reptile animals. Has a unique shape and building rules. Some reptile cards specifically require being placed in a reptile house rather than a standard enclosure.

Icons printed on specific spaces of your zoo map. When you place an enclosure tile covering a bonus icon, you immediately gain that reward (money, reputation, X-tokens, etc.). Planning placements to hit bonuses is a key spatial puzzle.

Each enclosure has a size from 1 to 5, determining which animals it can hold. An animal's size must exactly match its enclosure. Building larger enclosures requires higher Build action strength and costs more money.

A scoring track on the main board that increases when you play animals and certain sponsors. Higher appeal means more income during breaks. Appeal and Conservation tracks intertwine — when your markers cross, the game ends.

A scoring track on the main board that increases when you complete conservation projects and release animals. Runs opposite to the Appeal track. Your final score depends on where your markers cross on the intertwined track.

The section of the main board where the Appeal and Conservation tracks overlap and cross. When any player's two markers meet or pass each other on this track, the game ends immediately. Your position on this track determines your final score.

Calculated by the position where your Appeal and Conservation markers cross on the intertwined track, plus bonuses from your final scoring card. The further past the crossing point, the higher the score. Tiebreaker: most money, then most cards.

An income and upkeep phase triggered when the break token reaches the end of its track. All players receive income based on Appeal position and pay upkeep costs. Break token then resets. Not a round — breaks are event-driven.

Money received during a break, based on your position on the Appeal track. Higher appeal = more income. Income grows as you play more animals and sponsors, funding bigger plays in the mid and late game.
A continent icon found on animal cards from Africa. Collecting multiple Africa icons enables Africa-themed conservation projects and activates sponsor bonuses rewarding continent specialization.
A continent icon found on animal cards from Europe. Works with European partner zoos, conservation projects, and sponsor bonuses. European animals tend to be smaller but have useful abilities.
A continent icon found on animal cards from Asia. Enables Asian conservation projects and partner zoo synergies. Asia has a wide variety of animal types available.
A continent icon found on animal cards from North and South America. Works with Americas partner zoos and conservation projects. Includes diverse fauna from tropical to arctic species.

A continent icon found on animal cards from Australia and Oceania. Enables Australian conservation projects and partner zoo synergies. Australian animals are often unique species not found on other continents.

Icons on animal cards showing the creature's classification (primates, reptiles, birds, predators, herbivores, etc.). These icons are used for conservation project requirements, sponsor bonuses, and various scoring conditions.

The number on an animal card showing how much the Appeal track marker advances when that animal is played. Higher appeal value means the animal is a bigger draw for zoo visitors. Also printed on some sponsor cards.
When a card slides off position 6 of the display, any player may immediately pay 1 money to take it. Snapping happens instantly, even during another player's turn. A key way to acquire cards cheaply.

The row of 6 face-up zoo cards on the main board, numbered positions 1-6. When cards are taken from lower positions, remaining cards slide toward position 6. New cards fill from the deck. Cards pushed off position 6 can be snapped.
A marker on the break track that advances when certain events occur (display refills, card effects). When it reaches the end, a break (income/upkeep phase) triggers and the token resets.
Costs paid during a break phase. Covers the ongoing expenses of maintaining your zoo's animals and operations. Subtracted from your break income to determine net gain.
Your personal play area including your zoo map with enclosures and animals, played sponsor cards, partner zoo tiles, and university placements. Represents your entire zoo and its support network.
The maximum number of zoo cards you can hold in your hand. If you exceed the limit after drawing, you must discard down. Managing hand size is part of the Cards action decision.
Specific positions on the reputation track that unlock action card upgrades. When your reputation marker reaches a threshold, you immediately flip one action card from side I to side II.
